stem: The hypothalamus is located in a specific anatomical position relative to surrounding structures. Which description is MOST precisely stated?
options: Below the thalamus, between the optic tracts, anterior to the mamillary bodies, and superior to the pituitary gland
Below the thalamus, between the optic tracts, posterior to the mamillary bodies, and superior to the pituitary gland
Below the cerebral cortex, anterior to the optic chiasma, lateral to the mamillary bodies, and superior to the pituitary gland
Below the thalamus, at the optic chiasma, between the mamillary bodies, and posterior to the pituitary gland

stem: Specialized neurosecretory cells are concentrated within the hypothalamus. What is specifically stated about the precision of their localization before and after recent developments?
options: Before recent developments, the location of cell bodies was not precisely known except for the supraoptic and paraventricular nuclei; more recently, localization has become feasible with neuronal cells having identifiable areas
Before recent developments, the location was precisely mapped for all nuclei; more recently, new imaging technology has revealed that cell bodies are dispersed without identifiable areas
Before recent developments, only the paraventricular nuclei were localized; more recently, only the supraoptic nuclei have been precisely identified using advanced neuroimaging
Before recent developments, all hypothalamic nuclei were mapped precisely; more recently, some investigaors have questioned the accuracy of earlier localization studies

stem: CASE STUDY: A student lists the hypothalamic hypophysiotropic hormones. Which list is MOST completely and precisely stated without addition or omission?
options: Thyrotropin-releasing hormone (TRH), growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH), somatostatin (SS), gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH), corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH), prolactin inhibiting hormone (Dopamine), and sometimes prolactin-releasing hormone

Thyrotropin-releasing hormone (TRH), growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH), somatostatin (SS), follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH), prolactin inhibiting hormone (Dopamine), and prolactin-releasing hormone

Thyrotropin-releasing hormone (TRH), growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH), somatostatin (SS), gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH), cortisol-releasing hormone (CRH), dopamine, and sometimes prolactin-releasing hormone

Thyrotropin-releasing hormone (TRH), somatotropin-releasing hormone (SRH), somatostatin (SS), gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH), corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH), prolactin inhibiting hormone (Dopamine), and sometimes prolactin-releasing hormone

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q3): Among the listed hypothalamic hormones, which ONE is described as an amino acid-derived neurotransmitter rather than a peptide?
options: Somatostatin, because it is the only hypothalamic hormone that inhibits rather than stimulates the anterior pituitary
Dopamine, because apart from dopamine, all other hypothalamic hormones are peptides and can also act as neurotransmitters in other brain parts
Gonadotropin-releasing hormone, because GnRH is uniquely derived from amino acid precursors unlike TRH and CRH which are direct peptides
Thyrotropin-releasing hormone, because TRH is a tripeptide derived from amino acid precursors making it distinct from the other hypothalamic hormones

stem: The nerve fibres of hypothalamic neurosecretory cells terminate in the median eminence. This area has a specific vascular feature. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: The median eminence is richly endowed with a vascular connection with a primary network of capillary plexus formed by the superior hypophyseal artery
The median eminence is richly endowed with a vascular connection with a secondary network of capillary plexus formed by the inferior hypophyseal artery
The median eminence is richly endowed with a vascular connection with a tertiary network of venous sinuses formed by the superior hypophyseal vein
The median eminence is richly endowed with a direct arterial connection to the portal system formed by the inferior hypophyseal artery and superior sagittal sinus

stem: Hypothalamic hormones stimulate specific anterior pituitary cells. Which matching of hormone to stimulated cell type is MOST precisely and completely correct?
options: TRH stimulates thyrotropes; GHRH stimulates somatotropes; GnRH stimulates gonadotropes; CRH stimulates corticotropes; Dopamine inhibits lactotrophs
TRH stimulates corticotropes; GHRH stimulates thyrotropes; GnRH stimulates somatotropes; CRH stimulates gonadotropes; Dopamine inhibits lactotrophs
TRH stimulates thyrotropes; GHRH stimulates gonadotropes; GnRH stimulates somatotropes; CRH stimulates corticotropes; Dopamine inhibits thyrotropes
TRH stimulates lactotrophs; GHRH stimulates thyrotropes; GnRH stimulates corticotropes; CRH stimulates somatotropes; Dopamine inhibits gonadotropes

stem: CASE STUDY: A patient presents with anosmia, hypogonadism, cerebellar ataxia, sensorineural deafness, mental retardation, colour blindness, and physical defects. The nurse recognises this as consistent with which condition?
options: Generalised hypopituitarism caused by a pituitary adenoma compressing all anterior pituitary cells
Isolated congenital GnRH deficiency known as Kallmann's syndrome
Isolated congenital CRH deficiency leading to secondary adrenal insufficiency and hypocortisolism
Generalised hypothalamic dysfunction caused by craniopharyngioma compressing the entire hypothalamus

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q7): The nurse knows that primary target organ failure in this context causes a specific change in feedback. Which consequence is MOST precise?
options: Primary target organ failure results in loss of feedback inhibition on the hypothalamus, leading to excessive hormone production
Primary target organ failure results in increased feedback inhibition on the hypothalamus, leading to reduced hormone production
Primary target organ failure results in loss of feed-forward stimulation on the hypothalamus, leading to reduced hormone production
Primary target organ failure results in increased ultra-short loop inhibition, leading to excess hypothalamic releasing hormone secretion

stem: The pituitary gland is also known by another name and is located at a specific site. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: Also known as the hypophysis; located at the base of the skull in the sella turcica, a portion of the sphenoid bone named after the Turkish saddle, covered by a fold of dura matter called diaphragma sellae

Also known as the hypophysis; located at the base of the skull in the sella turcica, a portion of the ethmoid bone named after the Turkish saddle, covered by a fold of arachnoid matter called diaphragma sellae

Also known as the adenohypophysis; located at the base of the skull in the sella turcica, a portion of the sphenoid bone named after the Turkish saddle, covered by a fold of pia matter called diaphragma sellae
Also known as the hypophysis; located at the base of the skull in the sella turcica, a portion of the temporal bone, covered by a fold of dura matter called infundibulum sellae

stem: The pituitary gland has precise measurements. Which combination of size and weight is MOST accurately stated from the notes?
options: Measures 16mm x 11mm x 6mm and weighs 450-950mg; during pregnancy size increases by approximately 30%
Measures 16cm x 11cm x 6cm and weighs 450-950mg; during pregnancy size increases by approximately 30%
Measures 16mm x 11mm x 6mm and weighs 450-950g; during pregnancy size increases by approximately 50%
Measures 16mm x 11mm x 6mm and weighs 450-950mg; during pregnancy size decreases by approximately 30%

stem: CASE STUDY: A nursing student confuses the three lobes of the pituitary. Which statement MOST precisely identifies all three lobes and their status in humans?
options: The anterior pituitary (adenohypophysis), the posterior pituitary (neurohypophysis), and the intermediate lobe (pars intermedia) which is rudimentary in humans but much more developed in some lower animals
The anterior pituitary (adenohypophysis), the posterior pituitary (neurohypophysis), and the intermediate lobe (pars intermedia) which is fully functional in humans but rudimentary in lower animals
The anterior pituitary (adenohypophysis), the posterior pituitary (neurohypophysis), and the median eminence which is rudimentary in humans but much more developed in some lower animals
The anterior pituitary (adenohypophysis), the posterior pituitary (neurohypophysis), and the pars tuberalis which is rudimentary in humans but much more developed in some lower animals

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q11): A student asks about the structural components of the adenohypophysis and the neurohypophysis. Which is MOST precisely correct?
options: Adenohypophysis is made up of pars tuberalis and pars distalis; neurohypophysis consists of the neural stalk (infundibular stem) and pars nervosa (infundibular process)
Adenohypophysis is made up of pars tuberalis and pars nervosa; neurohypophysis consists of the infundibular stem and pars distalis
Adenohypophysis is made up of pars intermedia and pars distalis; neurohypophysis consists of the infundibular stem and pars nervosa
Adenohypophysis is made up of pars tuberalis and pars intermedia; neurohypophysis consists of pars distalis and pars nervosa

stem: The pituitary is connected to the hypothalamus by a tube-like structure. What is this structure called?
options: The pituitary stalk or the infundibular stem
The median eminence or the hypophyseal portal system
The infundibular process or the pars nervosa
The portal stalk or the hypophyseal tract

stem: The paraventricular and supraoptic nuclei of the hypothalamus have specific anatomical positions. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: Cells of the paraventricular nuclei are located on each side of the third ventricle; cells of the supraoptic nuclei are located above and lateral to the optic chiasma
Cells of the paraventricular nuclei are located on each side of the fourth ventricle; cells of the supraoptic nuclei are located below and medial to the optic chiasma
Cells of the paraventricular nuclei are located above the third ventricle; cells of the supraoptic nuclei are located below and lateral to the optic chiasma
Cells of the paraventricular nuclei are located lateral to the third ventricle; cells of the supraoptic nuclei are located anterior and medial to the optic chiasma

stem: CASE STUDY: A physiology student asks about the path taken by axons from paraventricular and supraoptic nuclei. Which is MOST precisely described?
options: The axons join to form a nervous tract and descend through the infundibular stalk to the posterior pituitary
The axons join to form a vascular tract and ascend through the infundibular stalk to the median eminence
The axons join to form a nervous tract and ascend through the pituitary stalk to the anterior pituitary
The axons join to form a portal tract and descend through the infundibular process to the posterior pituitary

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q15): The hypothalamus is under three types of feedback control. Which set is MOST precisely stated?
options: Long loop control by target hormones, short loop control by pituitary tropic hormones, and ultra-short loop control by the hypothalamic-releasing hormones themselves
Long loop control by hypothalamic hormones, short loop control by target hormones, and ultra-short loop control by pituitary tropic hormones
Long loop control by pituitary tropic hormones, short loop control by target hormones, and ultra-short loop control by hypothalamic-releasing hormones
Long loop control by target hormones, short loop control by hypothalamic-releasing hormones, and ultra-short loop control by pituitary tropic hormones

stem: Regulation of hormone secretion occurs mainly by which two primary mechanisms?
options: Feedback mechanisms (negative or positive) and sometimes by feed-forward means; regulation also occurs by degradation and excretion of the original hormone
Feedback mechanisms (negative only) and sometimes by feed-forward means; regulation also occurs by biosynthesis and storage of the original hormone
Feed-forward mechanisms and sometimes by feedback means; regulation also occurs by degradation and receptor down-regulation
Feedback mechanisms (negative or positive) and always by feed-forward means; regulation also occurs by protein binding and enzymatic conversion

stem: Feedback controls of hormone action serve a specific purpose. Which statement MOST precisely describes this?
options: Feedback controls predict changes in the secretory rate of hormones caused by over-secretion or under-secretion and modulate them to maintain the status quo
Feedback controls predict changes in the metabolic clearance rate of hormones caused by over-secretion or under-secretion and modulate excretion to restore normal levels
Feedback controls predict changes in the receptor density of target cells caused by over-secretion or under-secretion and modulate receptor synthesis to normalise response
Feedback controls predict changes in the biosynthetic rate of hormones caused by fluctuating substrate availability and modulate precursor uptake to maintain production

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse is explaining the negative feedback mechanism. Which statement is MOST completely and precisely grounded in the notes?
options: Negative feedback is the most typical method of regulation; the hormone in question causes a reduction in its own production; mechanisms may or may not involve the anterior pituitary; the hypothalamus produces a releasing hormone that stimulates the anterior pituitary to produce a trophic hormone that reaches the target organ; the target hormone negatively feeds back to the stimulating organ to inhibit hormone production

Negative feedback always involves the anterior pituitary; the hypothalamus releases a hormone that stimulates the anterior pituitary to produce a trophic hormone; the trophic hormone then feeds back to inhibit only the hypothalamus without affecting the anterior pituitary
Negative feedback involves only the target hormone directly inhibiting the hypothalamus; the anterior pituitary and pituitary trophic hormones are not involved in negative feedback inhibition
Negative feedback involves the anterior pituitary producing a hormone that inhibits the hypothalamus while simultaneously stimulating the target organ to produce more hormone

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q19): The nurse then explains the three levels of long-loop negative feedback inhibition. Which description is MOST accurately derived?
options: The target hormone negatively inhibits the production of its stimulating hormones â both the releasing hormone AND the trophic hormones; three levels are identified as the longest and most complex form
The target hormone inhibits only the trophic hormone from the anterior pituitary without affecting the hypothalamic releasing hormone level
The target hormone inhibits only the hypothalamic releasing hormone without affecting the anterior pituitary trophic hormone level
The target hormone inhibits the posterior pituitary neurohormones which then reduce hypothalamic releasing hormone output as the long loop

stem: Short loop negative feedback inhibition is described. Which statement is MOST precise?
options: The trophic hormone inhibits the production of its releasing hormone; this appears less important than the long-loop feedback mechanism; similar controls may exist for other hypothalamic hormones
The releasing hormone inhibits the production of its trophic hormone; this appears more important than the long-loop feedback mechanism
The target hormone directly inhibits the anterior pituitary trophic hormone without involving the hypothalamus; this is the most important feedback loop
The trophic hormone inhibits the target organ directly; this bypasses both the hypothalamus and the anterior pituitary

stem: Which specific example of short loop feedback is mentioned in the notes?
options: The effect of growth hormones on hormones that stimulate and inhibit growth hormones is a good example
The effect of cortisol on CRH production is a good example of short loop feedback
The effect of thyroid hormone on TSH production is a good example of short loop feedback
The effect of oestrogen on GnRH production is a good example of short loop feedback

stem: CASE STUDY: A student confuses ultra-short loop feedback with short loop feedback. Which description of ultra-short loop negative feedback inhibition is MOST precisely stated?
options: The releasing hormone inhibits its own production via autocrine or paracrine actions; evidence exists that vasopressin, oxytocin, and prolactin can control their secretions via this mechanism
The trophic hormone inhibits its own production at the anterior pituitary via autocrine actions; evidence exists that TSH and ACTH control their own secretion via this mechanism
The target hormone inhibits its own production at the target organ via autocrine actions; evidence exists that thyroid hormone and cortisol control their own secretion this way
The releasing hormone inhibits the trophic hormone production via paracrine actions within the median eminence; evidence exists that TRH and CRH use this mechanism

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q23): The other negative feedback mechanisms that do NOT involve the anterior pituitary are listed. Which list is MOST precisely and completely stated?
options: Regulation of insulin, vasopressin, oxytocin, catecholamines, aldosterone, parathormone, and calcitonin
Regulation of insulin, vasopressin, oxytocin, catecholamines, aldosterone, parathormone, calcitonin, and growth hormone
Regulation of insulin, vasopressin, adrenaline, aldosterone, parathormone, and calcitonin
Regulation of insulin, vasopressin, oxytocin, catecholamines, cortisol, parathormone, and calcitonin

stem: The positive feedback control mechanism is described. Which statement MOST completely and accurately reflects the notes?
options: In positive feedback, the result of the activity enhances the generation of more products; examples include high concentrations of oestradiol increasing LH pulse frequency and amplitude (LH surge), and uterine contraction causing cervical dilatation which stimulates more oxytocin production continuing until delivery of the baby
In positive feedback, the result of the activity suppresses further production; examples include high cortisol inhibiting CRH and ACTH to reduce further cortisol production
In positive feedback, the result of the activity maintains the status quo; examples include insulin reducing blood glucose back to normal fasting levels
In positive feedback, the result of the activity initially suppresses and then amplifies production; examples include the biphasic LH response to oestradiol

stem: CASE STUDY: A student asks to trace the milk rejection reflex as a positive feedback example. Which sequence is MOST accurately described in the notes?
options: Oxytocin causes the uterus to contract â further contraction â cervical dilatation â more oxytocin release â delivery of baby

Baby pushes against cervix causing it to stretch â stretching causes nerve impulses to be sent to the brain â brain stimulates pituitary to release oxytocin â oxytocin causes uterus to contract â cycle continues until delivery
Cervical dilatation stimulates oxytocin production â oxytocin causes uterine contraction â contraction stimulates further cervical dilatation â cycle continues until delivery
Uterine contraction stimulates the hypothalamus â hypothalamus releases oxytocin directly â oxytocin causes cervical dilatation â dilatation causes further contraction

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q26): In the feed-forward control mechanism, signals are sent to modulate events before they occur. Which example is MOST precisely stated from the notes?
options: Inhibition of the Purkinje cells by the basket cells in the cerebellum; both cells are stimulated through a common pathway; another example is regulation of heartbeat in advance in anticipation of exercise, or adjustment of posture in expectation of limb movement
Stimulation of the Purkinje cells by the basket cells in the cerebellum; each is stimulated through separate pathways; another example is the anticipatory increase in insulin secretion before a meal

Inhibition of the basket cells by the Purkinje cells in the cerebellum; both cells are stimulated through common pathways; another example is the anticipatory regulation of respiratory rate before strenuous activity

Stimulation of the basket cells by Purkinje cells in the cerebellum; both cells are inhibited through a common pathway; another example is regulation of cortisol in advance in anticipation of psychological stress

stem: Higher centres contribute to feedback control via specific structures. Which pair of structures is MOST precisely mentioned?
options: Via the limbic system and the cerebral cortex
Via the thalamus and the cerebral cortex
Via the limbic system and the basal ganglia
Via the reticular formation and the cerebral cortex

stem: CASE STUDY: A student is asked to list the stressful events that modulate hypothalamic function via higher centres. Which list is MOST precisely stated from the notes?
options: Emotions, sexual arousal, strenuous exercise, starvation, chronic and acute infections, and trauma; sometimes these can play overriding roles in negative feedback control mechanisms
Emotions, sexual arousal, strenuous exercise, starvation, and chronic infections; these never override negative feedback control mechanisms
Emotions, fear, sexual arousal, starvation, trauma, and inflammation; sometimes these play overriding roles in positive feedback mechanisms only
Emotions, sexual arousal, exercise, starvation, infections, and metabolic disorders; these always override negative feedback when they occur simultaneously

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q29): Autoregulatory mechanisms are mentioned. Which statement MOST precisely defines them?
options: Autoregulatory mechanisms involve the degradation of hormones
Autoregulatory mechanisms involve the amplification of hormones through second messenger cascades
Autoregulatory mechanisms involve the synthesis of hormones in response to feedback signals
Autoregulatory mechanisms involve the transport of hormones by plasma proteins

stem: The hypothalamic hormones are released in a specific pattern. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: The hormones are released in pulses; thus they exhibit the ultradian rhythm
The hormones are released in a constant tonic pattern; thus they maintain stable plasma levels throughout the day
The hormones are released in a circadian pattern; thus they exhibit the diurnal rhythm only
The hormones are released in response to single stimuli; thus they exhibit the infradian rhythm

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse explains the second messenger mechanisms used by hypothalamic hormones. Which combination is MOST accurately stated?
options: They act by binding to cell membrane receptors and elaborating calcium, phospholipids, or CAMP second messengers; the consequence is the activation of protein kinases
They act by diffusing directly into cells and binding to intracellular receptors to activate gene transcription without second messenger involvement
They act by binding to nuclear receptors and activating genomic processes; calcium is the exclusive second messenger used
They act by binding to cell membrane receptors and generating only cAMP as the exclusive second messenger

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q32): The consequence of protein kinase activation by hypothalamic hormone second messengers specifically results in stimulation of which anterior pituitary cells?
options: Thyrotropes, somatotropes, gonadotropes, corticotropes, and lactotrophs, respectively
Thyrotropes, somatotropes, gonadotropes, corticotropes, and melanotropes, respectively
Thyrotropes, corticotropes, gonadotropes, somatotropes, and lactotrophs, respectively
Thyrotropes, somatotropes, gonadotropes, corticotropes, and gonadotropes, respectively

stem: In addition to endocrine functions, the hypothalamus regulates several other physiological functions. Which set is MOST completely listed from the notes?
options: Behaviour, temperature, sleep-wake cycle, memory, satiety, thirst, and autonomic functions
Behaviour, temperature, sleep-wake cycle, mood, satiety, thirst, and neuroendocrine functions
Behaviour, temperature, circadian rhythm, memory, satiety, thirst, and immune functions
Behaviour, temperature, sleep-wake cycle, memory, hunger, thirst, and reproductive functions

stem: CASE STUDY: A patient presents with generalised total hypothalamic dysfunction. The nurse expects abnormalities in all the functions mentioned. Which statement MOST precisely reflects the notes about the consequence of generalised dysfunction?
options: A generalised total hypothalamic dysfunction will typically lead to abnormalities in all the above-mentioned functions
A generalised total hypothalamic dysfunction will typically lead to abnormalities in endocrine functions only, with preserved autonomic and behavioural functions
A generalised total hypothalamic dysfunction will typically lead to abnormalities in temperature and sleep-wake regulation only, as these are hypothalamic-exclusive functions
A generalised total hypothalamic dysfunction will typically lead to abnormalities in pituitary hormone production only, as the hypothalamus primarily regulates pituitary function

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q35): The notes state that hypothalamic dysfunction can be localised or generalised. A patient with isolated GnRH deficiency represents which type?
options: Localised dysfunction because only one specific hypothalamic function (GnRH production) is affected while other hypothalamic functions are preserved
Generalised dysfunction because isolated GnRH deficiency ultimately causes generalised pituitary failure over time
Localised dysfunction but only during childhood; it becomes generalised as the patient ages without treatment
Generalised dysfunction because GnRH controls gonadotropes which in turn regulate all anterior pituitary cell types

stem: The cerebral cortex regulates hypothalamic function via several inputs. Which inputs are MOST precisely listed?
options: Via the thalamus, limbic system, reticular activating system, and metabolic state of the body; afferent impulses are delivered via norepinephrine, serotonin, acetylcholine, amino acids, neurotransmitters, and other neuropeptides

Via the thalamus, limbic system, reticular activating system, and metabolic state of the body; afferent impulses are delivered via dopamine, serotonin, acetylcholine, amino acids, neurotransmitters, and other neuropeptides

Via the thalamus, limbic system, optic system, and metabolic state of the body; afferent impulses are delivered via norepinephrine, serotonin, acetylcholine, amino acids, neurotransmitters, and other neuropeptides

Via the thalamus, optic system, reticular activating system, and metabolic state of the body; afferent impulses are delivered via norepinephrine, dopamine, glutamate, amino acids, neurotransmitters, and other neuropeptides

stem: CASE STUDY: The example of high oestradiol causing the LH surge is described as positive feedback. Which feature of positive feedback MOST clearly applies here?
options: The result of the activity (high oestradiol) enhances the generation of more products (increased LH pulse frequency and amplitude) â consistent with positive feedback where outputs amplify further production
The result of the activity (high oestradiol) suppresses LH initially then amplifies it â this is a biphasic pattern unique to the ovarian-pituitary axis
The result of the activity (high oestradiol) maintains constant LH levels until ovulation occurs â consistent with the status quo maintenance function of feedback
The result of the activity (high LH) feeds back to increase oestradiol production â the causal direction flows from LH to oestradiol not the reverse

stem: The neurosecretory cells of the hypothalamus that synthesize hypophysiotropic hormones are located in the median eminence at a specific relationship to the third ventricle. This area has what unique property?
options: The median eminence is richly endowed with a vascular connection with a primary network of capillary plexus formed by the superior hypophyseal artery, facilitating direct hormonal transport to the anterior pituitary
The median eminence is richly endowed with a direct neural connection to the posterior pituitary via the infundibular tract, facilitating neurohormone transport by axoplasmic flow
The median eminence is richly endowed with a tertiary venous network formed by the inferior hypophyseal vein, facilitating portal blood delivery to the posterior pituitary
The median eminence is richly endowed with a secondary lymphatic network formed by the superior hypophyseal lymphatics, facilitating peptide hormone distribution to systemic circulation

stem: CASE STUDY: A 35-year-old woman presents with galactorrhoea and amenorrhoea. Her prolactin level is very high and her GnRH and LH are very low. A clinician explains this in terms of dopamine's role. Which explanation is MOST grounded in the notes?
options: Dopamine is the prolactin inhibiting hormone; reduced dopamine allows excess prolactin secretion; excess prolactin inhibits GnRH and gonadotropin secretion through a short-loop feedback mechanism
Dopamine is the gonadotropin stimulating hormone; reduced dopamine directly reduces GnRH production independent of prolactin levels
Dopamine is the thyrotropin releasing hormone antagonist; elevated prolactin competes with TSH at the receptor level to inhibit thyroid function
Dopamine directly inhibits GnRH release; elevated prolactin is a consequence of GnRH deficiency rather than a cause of it

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q40): The nurse explains that a single neuron or neurohormone from the hypothalamus can have two types of effects. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: A single neurohormone from the hypothalamus can control either excitatory or inhibitory effects on the pituitary
A single neurohormone from the hypothalamus can control only excitatory effects; inhibitory effects require a separate dedicated inhibitory hormone
A single neurohormone from the hypothalamus always exerts identical effects on all anterior pituitary cell types simultaneously
A single neurohormone from the hypothalamus controls only inhibitory effects through second messenger-mediated receptor down-regulation

stem: The neurosecretory cells of the hypothalamus produce hormones that are released into the portal system. What category of molecules are most of these hormones, with one exception?
options: Apart from dopamine (an amino acid-derived neurotransmitter), all others are peptides and can act as neurotransmitters in other brain parts
Apart from somatostatin (a cyclic peptide), all others are amino acid-derived neurotransmitters acting via second messengers
Apart from TRH (a tripeptide), all others are steroid hormones that diffuse across membranes to bind intracellular receptors
Apart from CRH (a large peptide), all others are small dipeptides that act via ion channel mechanisms

stem: CASE STUDY: A student is asked which neurotransmitters carry afferent impulses regulating hypothalamic function from the cerebral cortex. Which list from the notes must be precisely recalled?
options: Norepinephrine, serotonin, acetylcholine, amino acids, neurotransmitters, and other neuropeptides
Dopamine, serotonin, acetylcholine, amino acids, neurotransmitters, and other neuropeptides
Norepinephrine, dopamine, glutamate, amino acids, neurotransmitters, and other neuropeptides
Norepinephrine, serotonin, GABA, amino acids, neurotransmitters, and other neuropeptides

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q43): A clinical scenario involves a patient with trauma-induced hypothalamic dysfunction. Based on the notes, trauma can play an overriding role in which specific control mechanism?
options: Trauma can play an overriding role in negative feedback control mechanisms
Trauma can play an overriding role in positive feedback control mechanisms exclusively
Trauma can play an overriding role in feed-forward control mechanisms exclusively
Trauma can play an overriding role in autoregulatory mechanisms exclusively

stem: During pregnancy, the pituitary gland undergoes a specific change. What change is described?
options: Size increases by approximately 30%
Size increases by approximately 50%
Weight increases by approximately 30% but size remains unchanged
Size decreases by approximately 30% due to suppression of gonadotropes

stem: CASE STUDY: A student asks what the consequence of activating protein kinases in the anterior pituitary by hypothalamic second messengers ultimately is. What is MOST precisely stated?
options: The consequence is the stimulation of some anterior pituitary cells: thyrotropes, somatotropes, gonadotropes, corticotropes, and lactotrophs, respectively
The consequence is the stimulation of some anterior pituitary cells: thyrotropes, corticotropes, gonadotropes, somatotropes, and melanotropes, respectively
The consequence is inhibition of some anterior pituitary cells: lactotrophs, gonadotropes, corticotropes, somatotropes, and thyrotropes, respectively
The consequence is the stimulation of only corticotropes and gonadotropes while simultaneously inhibiting thyrotropes and somatotropes

stem: The pars intermedia is described as rudimentary in humans. What additional characteristic does the text mention about its development across species?
options: The pars intermedia is rudimentary in humans but much more developed in some lower animals
The pars intermedia is rudimentary in humans and equally rudimentary in all other mammals
The pars intermedia is rudimentary in humans but fully developed in other primates only
The pars intermedia is absent in humans and present only in reptiles and amphibians

stem: The hypothalamus is located below the thalamus, between the optic tracts and _______________ to the mamillary bodies.

CASE STUDY: An examiner asks: 'Hypothalamic neurosecretory cells synthesise hypophysiotropic hormones including thyrotropin-releasing hormone, growth hormone-releasing hormone, somatostatin, GnRH, CRH, and prolactin inhibiting hormone which is _______________.' One precise hormone name must be supplied.
Apart from dopamine, all other hypothalamic hormones are _______________ and can act as neurotransmitters in other brain parts.
CASE STUDY: An exam states: 'The hypothalamic hormones are released in pulses; thus they exhibit the _______________ rhythm.' One precise term must be supplied.
The pituitary gland is also known as the _______________ and is located at the base of the skull in the sella turcica.
CASE STUDY: An examiner states: 'The pituitary is covered by a fold of dura matter called _______________.' One precise anatomical term must be supplied.
The pituitary gland measures 16mm x 11mm x 6mm and weighs _______________ mg.

CASE STUDY: An exam question reads: 'The pituitary is connected to the hypothalamus by a tube-like structure called the pituitary stalk or the _______________ stem.' One anatomical term must be supplied.

The hypothalamus is under feedback control: long loop control by target hormones, short loop control by pituitary tropic hormones, and _______________ loop control by the hypothalamic-releasing hormones.
CASE STUDY: An examiner asks: 'In ultra-short loop feedback inhibition, the releasing hormone inhibits its own production via _______________ or paracrine actions.' One precise term must be supplied.
In positive feedback, the result of the activity _______________ the generation of more products.
CASE STUDY: An exam asks: 'Isolated congenital GnRH deficiency is known as _______________ syndrome.' One precise eponymous name must be supplied.
Feedback controls of hormone action predict changes in the secretory rate caused by over-secretion or under-secretion and modulate them to maintain the _______________ quo.
CASE STUDY: A student is asked: 'Cells of the paraventricular nuclei are located on each side of the _______________ ventricle.' One precise anatomical number or term must be supplied.
Cells of the supraoptic nuclei are located above and _______________ to the optic chiasma.
CASE STUDY: An examiner states: 'The adenohypophysis is made up of pars tuberalis and pars _______________.' One precise anatomical term from the notes must be supplied.
Regulation of hormone secretion also occurs by _______________ and excretion of the original hormone.

CASE STUDY: An exam asks: 'In the positive feedback example of parturition, uterine contraction causes cervical dilatation which stimulates _______________ production, leading to further contraction until delivery of the baby.' One hormone must be supplied.
Higher centres contribute to feedback control via the limbic system and the _______________ cortex.

CASE STUDY: A student is asked to recall which stressful events can play overriding roles in negative feedback. The notes list emotions, sexual arousal, strenuous exercise, starvation, chronic and acute infections, and _______________.
During pregnancy, the pituitary gland size increases by approximately _______________ percent.

CASE STUDY: An examiner asks: 'The nerve fibres of hypothalamic neurosecretory cells terminate in the median eminence of the hypothalamus located below the _______________ ventricle.' One anatomical term must be supplied.
Autoregulatory mechanisms involve the _______________ of hormones as one means of hormone regulation.
